pattern_recognition: This problem falls under the category of dynamic programming and string manipulation. It involves recognizing patterns in substrings and leveraging their properties to efficiently count palindromic sequences. Signals include the need to explore all possible substrings and check their properties.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Generate all possible substrings and check each one for being a palindrome.
approaches.algorithm: Iterate through all possible substrings, check if each substring is a palindrome, and count the valid ones.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use dynamic programming to expand around potential centers of palindromes, counting valid palindromic substrings efficiently.
approaches.algorithm: For each character and each pair of characters, expand outwards while checking for palindromic properties and count the substrings.

dry_run: Consider the input string 'aba'. Starting with the first character 'a', we check substrings: 'a' (palindrome), 'ab' (not a palindrome), 'aba' (palindrome). Next, we move to 'b': 'b' (palindrome), 'ba' (not a palindrome). Finally, we check the last 'a': 'a' (palindrome). Total palindromic substrings are 5: 'a', 'b', 'a', 'aba', 'a'.
edge_cases: An empty string should return 0. A single character string should return 1. Strings with all identical characters should return the maximum count of palindromic substrings. Large inputs may require efficient algorithms to avoid timeouts.
common_pitfalls: 1) Failing to check substrings of length 1, which are always palindromes. 2) Not considering even-length palindromes correctly. 3) Using a naive approach without optimizing for larger inputs, leading to performance issues.
